elements.sent-2411-001: We present the study of an end-to-end multi-track reconstruction algorithm for the central drift chamber of the \belletwo experiment at the \superkekb collider using Graph Neural Networks for an unknown number of particles.
elements.sent-2411-002: The algorithm uses detector hits as inputs without pre-filtering to simultaneously predict the number of track candidates in an event and and their kinematic properties.
elements.sent-2411-003: In a second step, we cluster detector hits for each track candidate to pass to a track fitting algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-004: Using a realistic full detector simulation including beam-induced backgrounds and detector noise taken from actual collision data, we find significant improvements in track finding efficiencies for tracks in a variety of different event topologies compared to the existing baseline algorithm used in \belletwo.
elements.sent-2411-005: For events with a hypothetical long-lived massive particle with a mass in the GeV-range decaying uniformly along its flight direction into two charged particles, the GNN achieves a combined track finding and fitting efficiency of 85.4\% with a fake rate of 2.5\%, compared to 52.2\% and 4.1\% for the baseline algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-006: This is the first end-to-end multi-track machine learning algorithm for a drift chamber detector that has been utilized in a realistic particle physics environment.
elements.sent-2411-007: These measurements are performed by tracking detectors that provide position measurements of energy depositions (or detector hits) left by charged particles ionizing material along their trajectories, commonly named tracks.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this paper, we describe a new track finding algorithm \cat~(CDC AI Track) using Graph Neural Networks~(GNNs) in the \belletwo central drift chamber~(CDC) to reconstruct charged tracks in electron-positron collisions.
elements.sent-2411-009: The \cat simultaneously detects an unknown number of objects, and infers their momenta and their point of origin.
elements.sent-2411-010: In a second step we associate detector hits to each of those objects that are then used as starting point for a subsequent conventional track fitting algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-011: We find significant improvements in track finding efficiencies for displaced tracks that originate from a position separated from the interaction point by a macroscopic distance of a few centimetres up to a meter.
elements.sent-2411-012: Such particles appear in theories beyond the Standard Model from decays of long-lived neutral mediators like dark photons~\cite{Ferber:2022ewf , or even displaced decay vertices that involve invisible particles in addition to a pair of charged particles~\cite{Duerr:2019dmv, Duerr:2020muu .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: At the same time, the efficiency and fake rate for prompt tracks from the electron-positron interaction point~(IP) is comparable to established methods in the central detector region but significantly better in the forward and backward detector regions.
elements.sent-2411-014: The \belletwo experiment is located at the high-intensity asymmetric $e^+e^-$ collider SuperKEKB in Tsukuba, Japan.
elements.sent-2411-015: SuperKEKB collides primarily 4\,\gev positron and 7\,\gev electron beams at a center-of-mass energy of the $\Upsilon(4S)$ resonance at approximately 10.58\,\gev to investigate rare B-meson decays and new physics phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-016: To achieve a higher sensitivity to very rare processes at the \belletwo experiment, SuperKEKB has the goal of increasing the instantaneous luminosity significantly compared to its predecessor, KEKB.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, this increase in luminosity also results in a significant increase in beam-induced background (called beam background in the following) that manifests in a high number of background detector hits, and a large number of charged and neutral background particles not originating from the IP~\cite{Natochii:2022vcs .}
elements.sent-2411-018: On average an $\Upsilon(4S)\to B\bar{B}$ decay in \belletwo will produce about 11 charged particles that typically feature momenta ranging from tens of MeV to a few GeV, while most direct searches for new physics feature lower charged track multiplicities.
elements.sent-2411-019: The paper is organized as follows: Section\,\ref{sec_related gives an overview of related work on machine learning~(ML) for track finding and end-to-end reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Section\,\ref{sec_cdc describes the \belletwo central drift chamber.}
elements.sent-2411-021: The event simulation and details of the beam background simulation and data are reported in Section\,\ref{sec_dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The metrics used to quantify track finding and track fitting performance are defined in Section\,\ref{sec_Metrics .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The existing and our new GNN-based track reconstruction algorithms are described in Section\,\ref{sec_reconstruction .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The main performance studies are discussed in Section\,\ref{sec_results .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The results are summarized in Section\,\ref{sec_conclusion .. \FloatBarrier.}
elements.sent-2411-026: While machine learning is widely used in high energy physics for event selection and analysis, the computationally intensive task of track reconstruction still largely relies on traditional algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-027: GNNs are recognized as a potential solution for handling irregular detector structures in high energy physics\,\cite{Shlomi:2020gdn .}
elements.sent-2411-028: GNN architectures in particular have the ability to learn a latent space representation of the detector structure itself~\cite{Wang:2018nkf, Qasim:2019otl , which is a key ingredient of the work presented in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-029: These architectures have been proven highly effective in handling the complex and irregular spatial structures of particle detectors, enabling more accurate and efficient analysis of high energy physics data.
elements.sent-2411-030: Graph segmentation in our work relies on object condensation\,\cite{Kieseler:2020wcq which has been used e.g. in end-to-end calorimeter reconstruction studies for the CMS HGCAL\,\cite{Qasim:2022rww .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the context of LHC track reconstruction in high pile-up events, the TrackML challenge~\cite{Amrouche:2019wmx, Amrouche:2021nbs has lead to a significant increase of development activities in the area of ML-based track reconstruction\,\cite{ExaTrkX:2020apx, Caillou:2024dcn, ExaTrkX:2021abe, Lieret:2023aqg .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The events from the TrackML challenge have a significantly higher number of tracks compared to \belletwo, a higher fraction of sensor hits belonging to signal tracks, and simpler track kinematics from particles produced at the interaction point with high transverse momentum.
elements.sent-2411-033: GNN-based tracking pipelines aiming for rather detector-unspecific solutions have been developed by the Exa.TrkX project for the HL-LHC\,\cite{ExaTrkX:2021abe , and by the ETX4VELO project for LHCb\,\cite{Correia:2024ogc .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Previous work has usually focused on simplified and idealised detector structures and simulations, and on tracks without significant displacement.
elements.sent-2411-035: Comparisons of GNN track finding to conventional algorithms in realistic HL-LHC scenarios have been shown for the ATLAS inner tracking pixel detector\,\cite{Caillou:2024smf .}
elements.sent-2411-036: GNNs for gaseous detectors have been studied for edge classification for the PANDA experiment\,\cite{Akram:2022zmj and BES~III\,\cite{Jia:2024rbx .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Utilizing deep learning techniques of semantic segmentation inspired by so called U-Nets, hit classification in high background environments has been demonstrated for the drift chamber of the COMET experiment ~\cite{Kaneko:2024ixs .}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, none of these works feature end-to-end ML-based solutions or conclusive studies of complex event typologies.
elements.sent-2411-039: Additional challenges arise from the differing input features between drift chambers as used in \belletwo, and the silicon pixels and strips used in HL-LHC.
elements.sent-2411-040: HL-LHC detectors, such as silicon pixel detectors \cite{Lieret:2023aqg, Lieret:2023ydc that provide 3D spatial information differ from drift chambers, which rely on indirect measurements of drift time and wire positions.}
elements.sent-2411-041: This makes track reconstruction in drift chambers more complex due to the lack of direct spatial information.
elements.sent-2411-042: In addition to GNNs, a wide range of ML-algorithms are currently being investigating for usage in track reconstruction, including e.g. large language models\,\cite{Huang:2024voo or transformers\,\cite{Caron:2024cyo .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Modern implementations of traditional track reconstruction algorithms are often enhanced with ML methods for specific tasks.
elements.sent-2411-044: For instance, the \belletwo experiment incorporates gradient boosted decision trees~\cite{Keck:2017gsv into its track reconstruction pipeline to improve beam background filtering and track-candidate search, and feed-forward neural networks for real-time reconstruction of the $z$ position in the CDC~\cite{Bahr:2024dzg .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: For an up-to-date list of works in particle physics that utilize ML, we refer to the living review \cite{hepmllivingreview .. \FloatBarrier.}
elements.section-2411-003: The Belle~II central drift chamber tracking detector
elements.sent-2411-046: The \belletwo detector is a charged particle spectrometer surrounded by particle-identification detectors, an electromagnetic calorimeter, and a $K^0_L$ and muon detector, arranged around the beam pipe in a cylindrical structure~\cite{Belle-II:2010dht .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The positive $z$ direction is pointing in the direction of the electron beam.
elements.sent-2411-048: The $x$ axis is horizontal and points away from the accelerator center, while the $y$ axis is vertical and points upwards.
elements.sent-2411-049: The longitudinal direction, the transverse plane with azimuthal angle $\phi$, and the polar angle $\theta$ are defined with respect to the detector’s solenoid axis.
elements.sent-2411-050: The charged particle spectrometer consists of silicon-based pixel and silicon-strip detectors that are not used for this work, and a gas-filled CDC.
elements.sent-2411-051: The CDC is 2.3\,m long, and has in total 14,336 sense wires and about 36,000 field wires forming drift cells with a size of about \hbox{$1\times1$\,cm$^{2}$} in the inner wire layers, to about \hbox{$2\times2$\,cm$^{2}$} elsewhere.
elements.sent-2411-052: The CDC covers the polar angle range $17^{\circ < \theta < 150^{\circ}$ and the full azimuthal angle $\phi$ range.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Particles with a polar angle between $17^{\circ < \theta < 35.4^{\circ}$ leave the CDC early in the forward endcap, particles with $35.40^{\circ < \theta < 123.04^{\circ}$ traverse the full detector, defined as barrel region, and particles $123.04^{\circ < \theta < 150^{\circ}$ leave the CDC early in the backward endcap.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The sense wires cover a radius between about 17\,cm to 110\,cm, and are arranged in 56 layers that are grouped in nine superlayers: the innermost superlayer consists of 8 layers with 160 sense wires each; the outer eight superlayers consist of 6 layers with 160 to 384 sense wires each.
elements.sent-2411-055: All superlayers alternate between wires aligned with the solenoid magnetic field, called axial layers A, and superlayers skewed by an angle between 66.8 and 74.1\,mrad in the positive and -58 to -78.6\,mrad in the negative direction, called stereo layers U and V.
elements.sent-2411-056: The resulting superlayer arrangement, numbered from inward to outward, is A1, U2, A3, V4, A5, U6, A7, V8, A9.
elements.sent-2411-057: The electrical field in each drift cell is approximately radial.
elements.sent-2411-058: The drift distance resolution of the CDC is about 120\,$\mu$m.
elements.sent-2411-059: A superconducting solenoid generates a magnetic field of about 1.5~T, which is directed along the central axis of the CDC support cylinder.
elements.sent-2411-060: Near the IP, there is a system of final focusing quadrupole and compensating solenoid magnets.
elements.sent-2411-061: The magnetic field is relatively uniform, with variations of approximately 1\% throughout the entire tracking volume.
elements.sent-2411-062: More details can be found in Ref.~\cite{BelleIITrackingGroup:2020hpx,Belle-II:2010dht,Kou:2018nap .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The CDC provides spatial information in the plane perpendicular to the sense wire axis, which aligns with the \belletwo $x-y$ plane for the axial layers and is tilted by their respective skew angles in the stereo layers.
elements.sent-2411-064: There is no information available for the $z$-coordinate on the wires, because the readout is only done on one side.
elements.sent-2411-065: This spatial information is encoded as the wire position and the signal's drift time (TDC), recorded when the energy deposition crosses the readout threshold, relative to an unknown global (common for all hits) time offset at the start of track finding.
elements.sent-2411-066: Additionally, the digitized signal amplitude~(ADC) and the time over the readout threshold~(TOT) are also provided.
elements.sent-2411-067: The digitized signal amplitude is proportional to the energy deposition of a particle.

elements.sent-2411-071: There are three key signatures with qualitatively different behaviour relevant for tracking:.
elements.sent-2411-072: Low transverse momentum tracks forming circles in the CDC ($p_{t \lesssim 0.4$\,GeV) versus high momentum tracks moving straight through the CDC ($p_{t > 0.4$\,GeV);.}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Particles traversing all CDC layers versus those exiting through the endcaps, creating shorter tracks;.

elements.sent-2411-124: For the evaluation of the track finding algorithms we first determine the hit efficiency and hit purity for each found track.
elements.sent-2411-125: The hit efficiency \hiteff per track is defined as the number of CDC hits matched to a simulated particle and included in a found track, divided by the number of all CDC hits matched to the same simulated particle in the whole event: \begin{equation}\hiteff = \frac{n_{\text{hits}}(\text{matched and} \in \text{track})}{n_{\text{hits}}(\text{matched})}\end{equation} A perfect hit efficiency is 1.0, indicating that all matched CDC hits are included in this track and no other found track contains hits matched to this simulated particle.
elements.sent-2411-126: The hit purity \hitpur per track is defined as the number of CDC hits matched to a simulated particle and included in a found track, divided by the number of all CDC hits included in the found track: \begin{equation}\hitpur = \frac{n_{\text{hits}}(\text{matched and} \in \text{track})}{n_{\text{hits}}(\in\text{track)}}\end{equation} A perfect hit purity is 1.0, indicating that all hits included in the found track are matched to the same particle.
elements.sent-2411-127: We use the hit efficiency and hit purity, and a minimal number of hits to define if a found track is related to a simulated particle: We require $\hiteff > 0.05$, $\hitpur > 0.66$, and $n_{\text{hits (\in\text{track)}\geq 7$.}}

elements.sent-2411-129: If more than one found track can be related to the same simulated particle, we choose the found track with the highest hit purity as matched to a simulated particle.

elements.sent-2411-131: If a track does not achieve the purity or efficiency requirements, it is defined as a fake track.
elements.sent-2411-132: If several tracks have the same hit purity, we choose the track with the highest hit efficiency as the correct match.

elements.sent-2411-144: Track reconstruction at \belletwo is performed in two steps.
elements.sent-2411-145: In the first step track finding algorithms assign tracking detector hits into subsets of hits belonging to the same charged particle.
elements.sent-2411-146: The track finding algorithms also provide a first estimate of the track kinematics.
elements.sent-2411-147: In a second step a dedicated track fitting algorithm is using these starting values and the set of identified hits to perform a track fit.
elements.sent-2411-148: Since the \cat performance is optimized using the track charge finding efficiency (see \cref{sec_Metrics ), we first describe the track fitting procedure, then the baseline finder and finally the \cat.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The track finding algorithms need to provide three sets of information to the subsequent track fitting algorithm:.
elements.sent-2411-150: The track fitting is performed using Kalman Filter algorithms implemented in GENFIT2~\cite{Hoppner:2009af,Rauch:2014wta,Bilka:2019ang, GENFIT_zenodo , that uses a Deterministic Annealing Filter~(DAF) to downweight hits far away from the fitted trajectory.}
elements.sent-2411-151: It is possible that a found track fails the track fitting if too many hits are rejected by the DAF.
elements.sent-2411-152: For all tracks in this study, we use a pion mass hypothesis during track fitting.
elements.sent-2411-153: The baseline track-finding algorithm is described in detail in Ref.~\cite{BelleIITrackingGroup:2020hpx and implemented in basf2.}
elements.sent-2411-154: The baseline track-finding algorithm is based on the Legendre transformation~\cite{Alexopoulos:2008zza with a main focus on tracks that originate from the close proximity of the IP (called \legendre in the following).}
elements.sent-2411-155: Hits with ADC and TOT values compatible with background hits are removed with minimal loss of signal hits in a pre-processing step.
elements.sent-2411-156: The \legendre is rather insensitive to missing hits in a track and starts by using axial-layers only to find 2D tracks in the $r$-$\phi$-plane.
elements.sent-2411-157: In a second step, hits in stereo layers are added that allow $z$ determination.
elements.sent-2411-158: A cellular automaton~\cite{Glazov:1993ur is used to find locally connected wire hits into track segments, followed by boosted decisions trees to add missing tracks-segments to tracks found by the \legendre.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The \legendre uses a fit of a two-dimensional circle to all identified axial hits to obtain an initial estimate of the track curvature and hence its transverse momentum.
elements.sent-2411-160: Using this previous 2D fit result, the initial estimate of the track longitudinal momentum is obtained from a linear fit to the track skew line in the $r$-$z$-plane.
elements.sent-2411-161: In the following section, we first describe the GNN architecture and the model inference of the \cat.
elements.sent-2411-162: We then describe the post-processing steps to choose the final track candidates and extract the track parameter information, the hits assigned to each tracks, and the hit ordering.
elements.sent-2411-163: Due to the sparsity of the wire hits in the CDC, with an average hit occupancy of up to 15\% per event for the high data beam backgrounds, the variable input size, and the non-uniform arrangement of the drift wires, we utilize a GNN architecture.
elements.sent-2411-164: The implementation of this GNN is done in \pyg~\cite{Fey/Lenssen/2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-165: Each node in the graph corresponds to a wire hit.
elements.sent-2411-166: The input features for our model are the $x$- and $y$-positions at the $z$ center of the corresponding sense wire, the layer, and superlayer, and layer within a superlayer information, the ADC count, and the TDC count information.
elements.sent-2411-167: Using cartesian coordinates as input features and prediction targets was crucial for the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-168: Polar coordinates and angle-based predictions caused issues due to the discontinuity when the angle resets from 360 degrees to 0, which the model struggled to learn effectively.
elements.sent-2411-169: Additionally, using the track helix radius as a training target proved ineffective, as it becomes nearly infinite for high-momentum, close to straight tracks, and very small for low-momentum tracks, making it impossible to scale the model prediction target to a reasonable range and therefore impossible for the model to learn.
elements.sent-2411-170: All input features except the position are normalized by dividing by their maximum to a range between 0 and 1.
elements.sent-2411-171: The ADC count is clipped to a maximum of 600 before normalization to remove the influence of rare anomalous values.
elements.sent-2411-172: For signal hits the majority of ADC counts is between 25 and 300.
elements.sent-2411-173: The objective of our algorithm is to provide the number of tracks, and the three-momentum, the starting point, the charge, and the hits associated with each track.
elements.sent-2411-174: Given the inherent challenge of not knowing the precise number of tracks in advance, we use an object condensation loss~\cite{Kieseler:2020wcq for this task.}
elements.sent-2411-175: The architecture of our model is illustrated in \cref{fig:architecture using \hbox{$N=4$} GravNet blocks~\cite{Qasim:2019otl .}}
elements.sent-2411-176: The output of each GravNet block is used for both the subsequent block and directly for the final layer through concatenation.
elements.sent-2411-177: In a final step, four parallel linear layers are responsible for generating the model's outputs as described in detail below.
elements.sent-2411-178: Each GravNet block starts with global average pooling \cite{globalpooling , where the mean value for each feature of the graph is calculated.}
elements.sent-2411-179: This averaged representation is then added to the original individual node features.
elements.sent-2411-180: This pooling technique enables the network to incorporate a collective understanding of the graph, complementing the information from individual nodes.
elements.sent-2411-181: This is followed by a sequence of two linear layers (LL), a batch normalization layer \cite{batchnorm and another linear layer.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Each of the linear layers use an exponential linear unit (ELU) activation functions \cite{elu .}
elements.sent-2411-183: The GravNet layer is responsible for building the graph and for message passing between nodes.
elements.sent-2411-184: In its first step, the GravNet translates the input features into learned representation spaces encoding spatial information, called $S$, and learned features $F_{LR}$.
elements.sent-2411-185: Undirected edges are then built between each node $j$ and its $k$ nearest neighbours in the representation space $S$ using an $n$-dimensional Euclidean distance $d=\sqrt{\sum_{i=1,..,n (X_{i,j - X_{i,k )^2}$, where $X_j$ is the position of the $j$-th node, and $X_k$ is the position of the $k$-th node.}}}
elements.sent-2411-186: The learned features of the connected nodes are weighted dependent on their Euclidean distance and then aggregated by a summation, resulting in updated features for each node.
elements.sent-2411-187: These features are concatenated with the initial node features.
elements.sent-2411-188: Following the GravNet layer, the feature extraction process continues with batch normalization.
elements.sent-2411-189: The resulting output is then forwarded to the next GravNet block and directly to the final linear layer, being passed through an extra linear layer (LL2) on this path.
elements.sent-2411-190: The four output layers in our model serve a dual purpose, addressing both object condensation and parameter prediction tasks.
elements.sent-2411-191: Each node of the graph is assigned one object to identify: if a wire hit is caused by an energy deposition of a signal particle, this node is assigned a unique integer particle ID$>0$.
elements.sent-2411-192: In contrast, if the hit is not created by a signal particle, an ID of 0 is assigned.
elements.sent-2411-193: We only use particles that have at least 7 matched hits in the event as signal particles.
elements.sent-2411-194: One output of the object condensation layers is a linear layer with a single node and a sigmoid activation function that generates a single output value $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-195: This $\beta$ value is used as a measure of a node being a condensation point.
elements.sent-2411-196: Another linear layer with the cluster space dimension of $CS=3$ output nodes provides coordinates within a learned cluster space for each node.
elements.sent-2411-197: This is also where the model learns to attract the nodes from the same objects together to the node with the highest $\beta$ value of the object and repels nodes that are from different objects.
elements.sent-2411-198: The primary objective is to have a single node with a high $\beta$ value per signal particle.
elements.sent-2411-199: For the track parameter prediction, we use a linear layer with three output nodes for predicting the three-momentum vector components for each node.
elements.sent-2411-200: A second linear layer with again three output nodes is used to predict the track starting position for each node.
elements.sent-2411-201: And finally, a linear layer with one output node and a sigmoid activation function is used for the charge prediction.
elements.sent-2411-202: The target truth information for these predictions is taken from the simulated particle matched to the node.
elements.sent-2411-203: The loss for the parameter prediction is weighed with the $\beta$ value, since we only require the actual condensation point to predict the particle parameters, condensing all information about the object on this one node.
elements.sent-2411-204: The total loss is then given by an unweighted sum of the different loss terms for the attraction, repulsion, $\beta$ value which includes a component to enforce only one condensation point per object and a component to suppress background, and the model parameter predictions.
elements.sent-2411-205: The details of the loss function are described in~\cite{Kieseler:2020wcq .}

elements.sent-2411-206: The procedure to retrieve the track information from the inference step of the trained model is shown schematically in \cref{fig:gnn_educational .}
elements.sent-2411-207: The following steps are performed in this order:.
elements.sent-2411-208: Each event is inferred by the model so each node has a predicted position in the latent cluster space, a $\beta$ value, and parameter predictions for all seven track parameters.
elements.sent-2411-209: We initiate the track finding process by introducing a threshold $t_{\beta}$,\ to the $\beta$ values resulting in condensation point candidates, as illustrated in \cref{fig:gnn_2 .}
elements.sent-2411-210: In the subsequent step we find isolated condensation points among the condensation point candidates: We compute distances $r$ between the condensation point candidate in the event with the highest $\beta$ value and all other condensation point candidates within the latent cluster space.
elements.sent-2411-211: We introduce distance threshold $t_d$, so that condensation point candidates located within a radius $r < t_d$ from the candidate with the highest $\beta$ value are removed.
elements.sent-2411-212: This process iterates until only condensation points remain, each separated by distances exceeding the radius $t_d$.
elements.sent-2411-213: The result of these operations is a set of condensation points as shown in \cref{fig:gnn_3 , each corresponding to a found track.}
elements.sent-2411-214: The parameters of each found track are given by the predicted momentum, position, and charge parameters of the respective condensation point (see \cref{fig:gnn_4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-215: To assign nodes to each condensation point we cluster nodes in the cluster space: we first calculate the distances between each condensation point and every other node within the cluster space.
elements.sent-2411-216: Any node with \hbox{$r < t_h$} is assigned to the found condensation point, shown in \cref{fig:gnn_5 .}
elements.sent-2411-217: We constrain the $t_h<t_d/2$, ensuring that each hit is used exclusively for at most one track.
elements.sent-2411-218: The found condensation point with its set of nodes is equivalent to a found track with assigned hits.
elements.sent-2411-219: To ensure that the found track can be fitted, we require at least seven hits assigned to the track, otherwise we reject the track and count it as not found.
elements.sent-2411-220: Finally, we order the assigned hits.
elements.sent-2411-221: The hits are ordered based on their positions in the $x$-$y$ plane of the detector.
elements.sent-2411-222: The process begins by selecting the hit closest to the predicted starting point if the starting point is within the CDC, or closest to the intersection between the predicted particle direction and the inner CDC cylinder surface if the starting point is before the CDC.
elements.sent-2411-223: We then calculate the Euclidean distance between the starting point $x$ and $y$ to the $x$ and $y$ positions for all hits.
elements.sent-2411-224: Subsequently, the closest hit to the previous one is determined iteratively until all hits are ordered.
elements.sent-2411-225: We note that this procedure is not working for low $p_t$ tracks that re-enter the CDC several times.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Track finding and track fitting efficiency
elements.sent-2411-257: The track finding efficiency (see \cref{eq:trackeff ) depends on the fraction of matched signal hits, the fraction of beam background hits that are wrongly assigned to the track, and wrongly assigned signal hits from other particles.}
elements.sent-2411-258: Particles that leave a small number of true signal hits are harder to reconstruct by the track finding algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-259: This affects particles with very small or very large polar angles in the forward or backward regions, or particle that are produced at a large distance from the IP.
elements.sent-2411-260: Particles that are close to other particles in real space often lead to a correlated efficiency loss, meaning that if one particle is lost, the other is lost, too.
elements.sent-2411-261: In our evaluation samples, this makes the light mass \dh samples the most challenging event samples for track finding.
elements.sent-2411-262: The baseline finder begins tracking in the $x-y$ plane, requiring a sufficient number of hits in the axial layers.
elements.sent-2411-263: This approach reduces efficiency for short tracks in the endcap regions.
elements.sent-2411-264: In the overall \belletwo tracking chain, efficiency is restored through the use of tracks identified by the silicon vertex detector.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, for displaced vertex signatures, the CDC remains the sole tracking detector, with no additional support to recover efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-266: We evaluate the track finding efficiency for prompt tracks using the track categories 1-3 (see \cref{tab:samples ).}
elements.sent-2411-267: The events have between 1 and 12 muons per event in the respective detector regions, with transverse momenta between $0.05 < p_t < 6 \,\gev$.
elements.sent-2411-268: Two categories of prompt tracks are particularly difficult in the \belletwo tracking environment:.
elements.sent-2411-269: Particles that re-enter the CDC several times without significant energy loss if their momenta is $p_t\lesssim 0.25\,\gev$ (so called inner curler), see \cref{fig:curler:inner ;.}
elements.sent-2411-270: Minimum-ionizing\footnote{Electrons, protons, and heavier mesons with such low transverse momentum usually loose too much energy in the calorimeter to produce a re-entering track.}
elements.sent-2411-271: We split the discussion in this subsection into tracks that are non-curling, and tracks that curl.
elements.sent-2411-272: To remove inner curler and outer curler, we exclude prompt particles with a distance between two consecutive hits larger than 20\,cm, and all particles with more than 32 signal hits in the CDC's first superlayer A1.
elements.sent-2411-273: We stress that we only remove such tracks from the evaluation, but not the events that contain these curlers.
elements.sent-2411-274: Particularly, the samples in category~1-3 contain many events with a large number of low transverse momentum tracks that occupy the inner part of the CDC.

elements.sent-2411-279: In the endcaps, the \cat has a significantly higher efficiency and charge efficiency and again higher fake and clone rates.

elements.sent-2411-390: On the other hand, it is rather straight forward to optimize the GNN for specific signatures if physics requirements can be restricted to certain decay topologies or momentum ranges.
elements.sent-2411-391: We have presented the implementation and a detailed study of the \cat~(CDC AI Tracking), an end-to-end multi-track reconstruction algorithm utilizing graph neural networks (GNNs) for the \belletwo central drift chamber.
elements.sent-2411-392: The \cat uses detector hits as inputs and simultaneously predicts the number of track candidates in an event, their associated hits, and their kinematic properties.
elements.sent-2411-393: We have used a full detector simulation and included beam backgrounds from actual collision data, and compared the GNN-based algorithm to the baseline track finding algorithm currently used in \belletwo for a wide range of event signatures, including tracks from decays that are macroscopically displaced from the interaction point.
elements.sent-2411-394: We find significant improvements in track finding efficiencies for displaced tracks in the barrel, and for both prompt and displaced tracks in the detector endcaps.
elements.sent-2411-395: The combined track-finding and fitting efficiency, as well as the low fake track rates for prompt tracks, are both comparable to the existing \belletwo track-finding method in the barrel region.
elements.sent-2411-396: To fully capitalize on the enhanced track-finding efficiency of the \cat, future adjustments to the track fitting procedure will be necessary.
elements.sent-2411-397: This work represents a significant conceptual step towards enabling end-to-end real-time GNN-based tracking on FPGAs \cite{Neu:2023sfh .}
elements.sent-2411-398: For real-time trigger applications, the clusterisation based on condensation points, the hit ordering in real space, and the track fitting step can be omitted if the inferred kinematics from the GNN provide sufficient resolution for trigger decisions.
elements.sent-2411-399: However, future work is needed on input quantization, pre-processing to remove beam background, as well as network size reduction.
elements.sent-2411-400: To our knowledge, the \cat is the first end-to-end machine learning tracking algorithm that has been utilized in a realistic particle physics environment, and the first completely GNN-based track finding in a drift chamber detector.

elements.sent-2411-414: The track parametrization follows a helix model, computed at the point of closest approach (POCA) to the collision point.
elements.sent-2411-415: We define the distance between the POCA and the collision point on the transverse (longitudinal) plane as $d_0$ ($d_z$), and the angle defined by the transverse (longitudinal) momentum at the POCA as $\phi$ ($\theta$).
elements.sent-2411-416: We evaluate the absolute residuals for these track features $\phi$, $\theta$, $d_0$, and $d_z$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:etap_trackparam} \eta(\phi,\theta,d_0,d_z) = (\phi,\theta,d_0,d_z)_{rec}-(\phi,\theta,d_0,d_z)_{simulated}\end{equation} for matched tracks.
elements.sent-2411-417: We then define the resolution $r(\phi,\theta,d_0,d_z)$ for the absolute residuals $\eta(\phi,\theta,d_0,d_z)$ as the 68\% coverage \begin{equation}\label{eq:resolution_trackparam} r(p_{\phi,\theta,d_0,d_z}) = P_{68\%} \left(\left|\eta - P_{50\%}(\eta)\right|\right),\end{equation} where $P_q$ is the $q$--th quantile of the distribution of $p_{t,z}$, and $P_{50\%}$ is the median of $\eta(p_{t,z )$~\cite{BelleIITrackingGroup:2020hpx .}}
